map chessdotcom rating to lichess

Lichess ratings are generally 

**200–400 points higher** than Chess.com ratings for most players. This is primarily because Lichess starts all players at 1500, while Chess.com typically starts them at 1200. ![Chess Stack Exchange](data:image/png;base64,)Chess Stack Exchange +4

Estimated Rating Conversion

While the gap narrows as you reach higher levels, the following general mapping applies to stable ratings:

-   **Beginner:** 800 (Chess.com) ≈ 1200–1300 (Lichess)
-   **Intermediate:** 1200 (Chess.com) ≈ 1500–1600 (Lichess)
-   **Advanced:** 1800 (Chess.com) ≈ 2100 (Lichess)
-   **Elite:** Ratings typically converge around **2200–2400**. At the Grandmaster level, Chess.com ratings can actually be higher than Lichess. ![Reddit](data:image/png;base64,)Reddit +8

Key Differences by Time Control

According to data from [ChessGoals](https://chessgoals.com/rating-comparison/): 

-   **Blitz:** Lichess ratings are about 40 points higher than they were previously relative to Chess.com.
-   **Classical:** Lichess is roughly 20 points higher than its previous relative standing.
-   **Bullet:** Lichess ratings are often closer to Chess.com or slightly lower than other time controls. ![ChessGoals Courses](https://encrypted-tbn0.gstatic.com/faviconV2?url=https://chessgoals.com&client=AIM&size=128&type=FAVICON&fallback_opts=TYPE,SIZE,URL)ChessGoals Courses

Why the ratings differ

-   **Starting Point:** Lichess uses 1500 as the baseline; Chess.com uses 1200.
-   **System:** Lichess uses the **Glicko-2** rating system, while Chess.com uses a variation of **Glicko-1**.
-   **Player Pool:** Chess.com has a larger, more casual player base, which compresses the rating distribution. ![Reddit](data:image/png;base64,)Reddit +4

For a more precise estimate based on your specific stats, you can use the Chess Rating Comparison tool or the [Universal Rating Converter](https://lichess.org/@/NoseKnowsAll/blog/introducing-a-universal-rating-converter-for-2024/X2QAH27t). 

Would you like to know how these online ratings compare to official **FIDE** or **USCF** tournament ratings?
